projects.title: Aero — U-Robot Link Universal Robot Controller
projects.desc:
Aero — U-Robot Link

A universal Android robot controller app published on the Google Play Store, designed to eliminate the need to build a custom remote-control app for every new robotics project. One app. Every platform.

Overview

Aero was built to solve a recurring problem in the maker and robotics community — every new project meant reinventing the same controller UI from scratch. Aero replaces that by providing a single, polished Android app that speaks a simple serial protocol over Bluetooth, BLE, or WiFi, and automatically translates it into the correct binary protocol when connecting to LEGO MINDSTORMS hardware.

Whether you're driving an Arduino car, monitoring IoT sensors, or controlling a LEGO EV3 robot — Aero handles it all without writing a single line of controller code.

Key Features

📡 Multi-Protocol Connectivity     Supports Bluetooth Classic (HC-05/HC-06), BLE (HM-10, ESP32), and WiFi TCP (ESP8266, ESP32) — all from the same device connection screen. Includes auto-reconnect on startup for frequently used devices.

🤖 LEGO MINDSTORMS Support (NXT & EV3)     Connects natively to LEGO NXT and EV3 bricks using the official LEGO Direct Commands protocol over Bluetooth — no custom firmware, no ev3dev, no leJOS required. Motor port mapping, directional inversion, brake-on-stop, deadband, and max power are all configurable from within the app.

🎮 10 Control Modes     Includes a fully customizable button grid, dual joystick with differential drive math, tilt control via accelerometer, gamepad support for PS4/PS5/Xbox controllers, RGB color picker, serial terminal, countdown timer with auto-send, voice control via Google Speech Recognition, a live IP camera viewer (MJPEG), and a full IoT sensor dashboard.

📊 IoT Sensor Dashboard     Parses SENSOR:key:value messages from any connected device and auto-generates live widgets — gauge, chart, bar, LED indicator, compass, and more — with color-coding, threshold alerts, drag-to-reposition layout, and profile saving. Supports up to 500 readings per sensor in memory.

🌐 Built-in Web Dashboard     Runs a local WebSocket server on port 8080 that lets any browser on the same WiFi view live sensor data, sparkline charts, history tables, and CSV exports — no cloud, no subscription, fully offline.

📱 Live Camera Stream     Streams MJPEG video from ESP32-CAM or any compatible IP camera directly in the app, operating independently from the robot control connection.

🧩 Saved Profiles & Dark Mode     Multiple named profiles per device for switching between control layouts (e.g. "Drive Mode", "Arm Mode", "IoT Mode"). Full light/dark theme support for outdoor and low-light use.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
PlatformAndroid (Java / Android SDK, API 24+)
ConnectivityBluetooth Classic · BLE · WiFi TCP
LEGO ProtocolLEGO Direct Commands (NXT & EV3 binary spec)
CameraMJPEG stream rendering
VoiceGoogle Speech Recognition API
Web ServerLocal WebSocket + HTTP server
DistributionGoogle Play Store

projects.title: QR Attendance System
projects.desc:
QR-SMS Attendance System

A desktop attendance management application built with C# and Windows Forms, designed to streamline student check-in/check-out using QR code scanning and automated SMS notifications to guardians.

Overview

This project addresses a common pain point in school administration — manual attendance tracking is slow, error-prone, and leaves parents out of the loop. The QR-SMS Attendance System replaces that process with a real-time, camera-based solution that logs attendance and immediately notifies a student's guardian via SMS the moment they arrive or are dismissed.

Key Features

📷 QR Code Scanning     Uses the device's webcam to continuously scan for student QR codes in real time, with a live camera feed, bounding-box overlay on successful scans, and intelligent decode throttling to keep CPU usage low.

📋 Student Database Integration     Loads a student roster directly from an Excel spreadsheet (.xlsx), automatically mapping columns for name, LRN, phone number, address, year level, section, track/strand, guardian, and more. No manual re-entry required.

📲 Automated SMS via Webhook     On a successful scan, the app builds a personalized SMS message and dispatches it to a connected Android device via a webhook endpoint. Messages are fully customizable with template placeholders and include a 5-second cooldown per student to prevent duplicate sends.

⏱ On-Time / Late Detection     Compares the scan timestamp against a configurable scheduled time and automatically stamps each log entry as On Time, Late, or Dismissed for OUT scans.

📁 CSV Attendance Logging     Every scan is written to a persistent attendance.csv file with full student details, timestamp, and IN/OUT status — ready for reporting or further processing.

🔲 QR Code Generator     Built-in tool to generate and save QR codes from any text input, making it easy to produce student ID QR codes directly within the same application.

Tech Stack

CategoryTools Used
Language / FrameworkC# · .NET · Windows Forms
Computer VisionOpenCvSharp
QR Decoding / EncodingZXing.Net
Excel I/OClosedXML
MessagingHTTP Webhook (JSON → Android SMS Bridge)
Data StorageCSV · Plain Text

projects.title: Spongebot
projects.desc:
Spongebot

A SpongeBob SquarePants-themed Arduino robot built as a personal gift, controlled wirelessly via a custom Android app over Bluetooth. A passion project that combines embedded hardware, custom industrial design, and mobile app development into one character-accurate, fully functional robot.

Overview

Spongebot started as a personal project — a one-of-a-kind SpongeBob robot built and gifted to someone special. Rather than using an off-the-shelf controller, a dedicated Android app was designed and built specifically for it, giving the robot a polished user experience that matched its personality. The result is a fully self-contained system: power it on, open the app, and it connects automatically.

Key Features

📱 Dedicated Android App     A custom-built Android application (.apk) designed exclusively for Spongebot. The app auto-connects to the robot via Bluetooth on launch — no manual pairing steps needed after the first setup.

🔵 Bluetooth Wireless Control     Communicates over Bluetooth Classic using an HC-05 module. A built-in LED indicator on the robot provides clear connection feedback — blinking red means not connected, steady red means connected and ready.

🎨 Custom Physical Design     The robot body was designed and built to resemble SpongeBob, with a custom layout crafted to match the character's proportions and look. Design files are included in the repository under the sponge layout folder.

⚡ Arduino-Powered Firmware     The robot's onboard logic runs on Arduino firmware written in C/C++, handling Bluetooth command parsing and motor/actuator control. Circuit schematics and Fritzing diagrams are included for full reproducibility.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
MicrocontrollerArduino
FirmwareC / C++ (Arduino IDE)
WirelessBluetooth Classic (HC-05)
Mobile AppAndroid (Custom APK)
Circuit DesignFritzing
Physical DesignCustom layout / enclosure

projects.title: QR Scanner Maker Plus Ultra
projects.desc:
QR Scanner Maker Plus Ultra

A 3-in-1 Android QR and barcode utility app published on the Google Play Store, combining real-time camera scanning, photo-based QR reading, and QR code generation into a single lightweight tool.

Overview

Most QR apps do one thing — scan. QR Scanner Maker Plus Ultra does three. Whether you need to scan a code in real time, decode a QR from an existing photo in your gallery, or generate a brand-new QR code from any text or URL, this app handles all of it from a single interface. Two of the three features work fully offline, making it reliable even without a data connection.

Key Features

📷 Real-Time QR & Barcode Scanner     Uses the device camera to instantly scan and decode QR codes and barcodes in real time. Works entirely offline — no internet connection required.

🖼️ Scan QR from Photos     Reads and decodes QR codes directly from images stored in the device gallery. Useful for codes that have already been photographed or saved as screenshots. Also works offline.

✨ QR Code Generator     Generates a QR code from any text, link, or data input. Allows users to instantly create shareable QR codes for their own content.

📦 3 Releases on GitHub     Actively maintained with 3 published releases, with the latest version (v1.0.2) released in January 2024.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
PlatformAndroid
DistributionGoogle Play Store
FrontendHTML (WebView-based)
FeaturesCamera scanning · Gallery image decoding · QR generation

projects.title: Portable RetroPie Mini Arcade
projects.desc:
Portable RetroPie Mini Arcade

A handheld retro gaming console built from scratch using a Raspberry Pi 4, a 7-inch touchscreen, and a custom-designed enclosure — capable of emulating dozens of classic game consoles in a portable, self-contained form factor.

Overview

This project takes the open-source RetroPie platform and packages it into a compact, purpose-built arcade cabinet you can carry anywhere. Rather than using a generic case, the enclosure was designed from scratch with precise dimensions, fabricated in acrylic, and also released as a fully printable 3D STL file on Thingiverse — so anyone can replicate the build. The result is a portable gaming machine that plays classic titles from NES, SNES, PS1, Game Boy, and dozens of other platforms, all on a 7-inch touchscreen with built-in audio.

Key Features

🖥️ 7-Inch Capacitive Touchscreen Display     Powered by the Waveshare 7-inch HDMI LCD (1024×600 resolution) with capacitive touch support. Connected to the Raspberry Pi 4 via Micro HDMI, delivering a crisp, responsive display for both gaming and navigation through the RetroPie menu.

🎮 Raspberry Pi 4 Brain     Built on the Raspberry Pi 4 (2GB), offering more than enough processing power to run emulators for older consoles smoothly. Supports both wired USB joystick controllers and Bluetooth gamepads for flexible control options.

🔊 Built-in Audio     An 8Ω 5W speaker mounted at the back of the LCD screen provides onboard audio — no external speaker or headphones required.

🔋 Fully Portable     Powered entirely by a powerbank, making the unit wireless and play-anywhere without needing a wall outlet. The Raspberry Pi and the display each draw from the same bank, keeping the setup clean and cable-minimal.

📦 Custom-Designed Enclosure     The housing was designed with exact dimensions (275mm × 158mm front/back, 132mm depth) and built using acrylic panels and angle brackets. A 3D-printable STL version of the full enclosure was also published on Thingiverse for the community to reproduce.

🕹️ RetroPie Software Stack     Runs RetroPie on a 64GB SD card, pre-loaded with a full multi-console image (Dual Screen Raspberry Pi4 RetroPie "CRT BLAST" build from ArcadePunks). Supports ROMs from a wide range of classic platforms, configured with the correct emulators and BIOS files out of the box.

⚙️ Optional Desktop PC Mode     With an optional Raspbian OS setup, the same unit can boot into a full Raspberry Pi desktop environment — functioning as a portable mini-PC for browsing, documents, and more when not gaming.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
ComputingRaspberry Pi 4 Model B (2GB)
DisplayWaveshare 7" HDMI LCD · 1024×600 · Capacitive Touch
OS / EmulationRetroPie (Linux-based)
Storage64GB SD Card
Audio8Ω 5W Speaker
PowerPortable Powerbank
ControlsUSB Joystick Controller / Bluetooth Gamepad
EnclosureAcrylic (hand-built) + 3D-printable STL (Thingiverse)

projects.title: Wireless IR Temperature Scanner
projects.desc:
Wireless IR Temperature Scanner

A contactless Bluetooth temperature scanner built with Arduino and an MLX90614 infrared sensor, designed to allow safe, distance-based body temperature readings viewed in real time through a custom Android app. Published on Hackster.io and the Google Play Store.

Overview

This project was built as a practical response to the need for non-contact fever screening — a situation where a person monitoring temperatures at a building entrance has to stand close to each individual, raising their own risk of exposure. The Wireless IR Temperature Scanner solves this by placing the sensor device near the entrance while the operator monitors readings from up to 9 meters away via Bluetooth on their phone.

"Touch Less and Do More."

Key Features

🌡️ Non-Contact Infrared Sensing     Uses the MLX90614 infrared thermometer sensor, which reads both ambient (room) and object (body) temperatures without any physical contact. Accuracy is ±0.02°C over an object temperature range of -70°C to 382.2°C — well within the precision needed for human fever screening.

📡 Wireless Bluetooth Transmission     Temperature data is transmitted wirelessly via an HC-06 Bluetooth module with a range of up to 9 meters, allowing the monitoring operator to maintain a safe distance from the person being screened.

📱 Dedicated Android App (Google Play Store)     A companion app — Wireless IR Temperature Scanner Plus ULTRA — was built and published to the Google Play Store. It pairs with the device over Bluetooth and displays live ambient and object temperature readings with color-coded fever thresholds: Normal (34.8°C – 37.3°C), High (37.4°C – 38°C), and Fever (38.1°C+).

🔋 Portable & Self-Contained     Powered by a standard 9V battery with an on/off rocker switch. The circuit was hand-soldered onto a universal PCB with female pin headers for modular assembly, making it easy to modify or repair.

📖 Fully Documented & Published     Complete build documentation — including schematics, wiring diagrams, firmware, and step-by-step instructions — was published on both Hackster.io and Instructables, accumulating over 11,000 views on Hackster alone.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
MicrocontrollerArduino Nano
Temperature SensorMLX90614 (I2C, non-contact IR)
Wireless ModuleHC-06 Bluetooth Classic
FirmwareC++ (Arduino IDE · Adafruit MLX90614 Library)
Mobile AppAndroid (Google Play Store)
PCBHand-soldered universal PCB
Power9V battery

projects.title: WiFi Default Password Plus ULTRA
projects.desc:
WiFi Default Password Plus ULTRA

An Android utility app published on the Google Play Store that generates the default WiFi password for common Philippine ISP routers — particularly PLDT — based on the network's SSID or MAC address. A practical tool for users who've forgotten their router's factory password and need to recover it without a reset.

Overview

Many home routers in the Philippines ship with passwords algorithmically derived from their SSID or MAC address. This project reverse-engineers those generation algorithms and wraps them into a clean, easy-to-use Android app. Instead of manually performing hex conversions or multiplications by hand, users simply input their network details and the app instantly calculates the likely default password. The app covers 8 distinct router SSID types across PLDT Fibr, PLDT Home DSL, and HomeBro networks.

Key Features

🔐 Multi-Algorithm Password Generation     Each supported router type uses a different algorithm, all implemented within the app: hex complement conversion (for PLDTFIBR and PLDTHOMEFIBR variants), MAC address suffix extraction (for PLDTHOMEDSL and HomeBro), and hexadecimal-to-decimal multiplication (for numeric and alphanumeric DSL SSIDs).

📶 Supports 8 SSID Types     Covers the most common PLDT router variants used across the Philippines, including PLDTFIBR_xxxxxx, PLDTHOMEFIBRxxxxx, PLDTHOMEDSL, PLDTmyDSLPAL, PLDmyDSLBiz, PLDTHOMEDSLxxxxx (numeric and alphanumeric), and HomeBro_Ultera.

📱 Published on Google Play Store     Available as a free download with 2 released versions (v1.3 latest), accumulating 9 stars and 3 forks on GitHub — demonstrating real community adoption and interest.

⚡ Instant, Offline Generation     All password generation logic runs entirely on-device with no internet connection required. Users input their SSID or MAC address and get the computed default password immediately.

Supported Algorithms

SSID FormatAlgorithm
PLDTFIBR_xxxxxxLast 6 of SSID → hex complement → prepend wlan
PLDTHOMEFIBRxxxxxLast 6 of SSID → hex complement → prepend PLDTWIFI
PLDTHOMEDSL / PLDTmyDSLPALLast 5 of MAC address → prepend PLDTWIFI
HomeBro_UlteraLast 6 of MAC address → prepend HomeBro_
PLDTHOMEDSLxxxxx (numeric)Last 5 digits × 3 → prepend PLDTWIFI
PLDTHOMEDSLxxxxx (alphanumeric)Last 5 (hex → decimal) × 3 → prepend PLDTWIFI

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
PlatformAndroid
DistributionGoogle Play Store
LogicSSID/MAC-based password generation algorithms
LanguageJava (Android SDK)

projects.title: Arduino Piano With Manual & 7 Preset Songs
projects.desc:
Arduino Piano With Manual & 7 Preset Songs

A dual-mode electronic piano built with Arduino, featuring a 7-key manual playable keyboard and a preset song player with 7 built-in songs, all displayed on an LCD screen. Published as a full step-by-step tutorial on Instructables.

Overview

This project turns a handful of pushbuttons and a passive buzzer into a fully functional mini piano — with two distinct operating modes. In Manual Mode, each of the 7 buttons plays a musical note, letting you play freely by ear. Switch over to Preset Mode and the piano becomes a song player, cycling through 7 pre-programmed songs with a single button press. An LCD display shows the current mode and active song, giving the build a clean, finished feel beyond a bare breadboard prototype.

Key Features

🎹 7-Key Manual Piano Mode     Seven pushbuttons mapped to the seven musical notes (Do–Re–Mi–Fa–Sol–La–Si / C–D–E–F–G–A–B). Press any key and the buzzer plays the corresponding frequency in real time, functioning as a fully playable mini keyboard.

🎵 7 Preset Songs Mode     A dedicated mode button switches the device into Preset Mode, disabling manual play and enabling a song menu. Each subsequent press of the mode button cycles through 7 pre-programmed songs that play automatically — full melodies encoded note-by-note in the firmware.

🖥️ LCD Display Interface     A 16×2 LCD screen displays the current operating mode and the active preset song title, giving real-time feedback and making the device easy to navigate without needing to count button presses.

📖 Published on Instructables     Documented as a complete 7-step tutorial with wiring diagrams, circuit schematics, and full source code — making it fully reproducible by the maker community.

Tech Stack

CategoryDetails
MicrocontrollerArduino
OutputPassive Buzzer
Display16×2 LCD
Input7× Pushbuttons (notes) + 1× Mode Button
FirmwareC++ (Arduino IDE · pitches.h library)
DocumentationInstructables (7-step tutorial)
